beats.text: You made graphs with scale of 5. Let's connect what you learned to the world around you.

incorrects.description: Student has not selected all correct answers (first attempt)

steps.text: Look at each image carefully. Do they all show groups of 5?

incorrects.description: Student has not selected all correct answers (second attempt)

params.description: All four images pulse to draw attention.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count the fingers on a hand. Look at how tally marks group. Check what a nickel is worth. Count the spaces between clock marks.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: All four images highlight in sequence, showing the 5s connection.

steps.text: Let me show you. A hand has 5 fingers. Tally marks bundle by 5s. A nickel equals 5 cents. A clock face marks every 5 minutes. All four use groups of 5. That's what makes counting by 5s so useful. It shows up everywhere.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2, thinking it needs fewer symbols

steps.text: Think about how many symbols you'd need. With Scale of 2, each symbol is 2 items. How many symbols would you need to show 30 items?

incorrects.description: Student selected they'd need the same number of symbols

steps.text: Let's compare. Scale of 2 means each symbol stands for 2 items. Scale of 5 means each symbol stands for 5 items. Which one covers more items per symbol?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

steps.text: Let me show you how to think about this. With Scale of 2, each symbol is 2 items, so 30 items needs 15 symbols. With Scale of 5, each symbol is 5 items, so 30 items needs only 6 symbols. The bigger the scale, the fewer symbols you need. The answer is Scale of 5.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Representative data from lesson. Scale of 5 visible in key.
Horizontal bar graph appears alongside picture graph. Same representative data. Vertical axis shows scale of 5.

beats.text: You started this unit knowing scale of 2. Now you can make graphs with scale of 5 too.
And you made BOTH picture graphs AND bar graphs. Two formats, both showing the same data.
Two scales in your toolkit. Two formats you can create. You're ready for more.
You started with scale of 1, then 2, now 5. Next time, we'll add scale of 10 to your toolkit.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Representative lesson data with scale of 5.
Horizontal bar graph alongside picture graph. Same data, scale of 5 on vertical axis.
